Justification: The study frames functional redundancy and response diversity as indicators of ecosystem resilience and tests whether pre-disturbance FR and RD predict post-logging productivity. Resilience is therefore evaluated through proxy indicators rather than being directly measured as system recovery. The methods state that FR and RD were measured pre-disturbance in sample plots and were not remeasured after clear cutting. Post-disturbance productivity was measured as seasonal variation in enhanced vegetation index from MODIS and analyzed in autoregressive regression models as the response variable. Because resilience itself is not quantified from a recovery trajectory but inferred from diversity indicators FR and RD the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The target variables used to infer resilience are FR and RD which are functional diversity indicators so the target variable type is diversity. These diversity metrics are calculated from multiple tree species within plots so the level is community. Data sources are temporary sampling plots and MODIS satellite imagery which are field observation. The first author affiliation is Laval University in Quebec Canada and the study area is forests of Quebec Canada. The paper provides a map but no coordinates so location and location range are NA. The focal organisms are trees so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because the measurement is inferred.

The study states that a field experiment was conducted to quantify soil microbial responses and resilience to precipitation changes with nitrogen addition. Five precipitation levels were applied in 2016 to 2018 and then reversed in 2019 to recover to the levels expected over 4 years. Resilience was defined as the ratio of the soil microbial traits after the reverse treatments to those of the control treatment. This uses observed soil microbial traits measured by PLFA biomass markers for bacteria fungi actinomycetes GP GN AMF and protozoa and ratios GP GN and F B. The discussion states resilience can incorporate both resistance and recovery. The first author affiliations are in Beijing China. The experimental site is Bailingmiao Damao County Inner Mongolia China with coordinates 41 38 38.300 N and 110 13 53.300 E. The ecosystem is described as a desert steppe and the vegetation mainly consists of native grasses. Disturbances are manipulated precipitation regime changes and manipulated nitrogen addition as ammonium nitrate. The precipitation and nitrogen treatments were maintained across years with reversal in the final year so the disturbance is treated as press. Soil samples for microbial traits were taken in late August 2018 and late August 2019 spanning the 4 year experiment from early May 2016 to late August 2019.

The study used 64 years of stand productivity measures and measured annual growth increments of over 900 trees with productivity defined as annual basal area produced and community productivity as the sum within a stand. Resilience is explicitly defined as the speed at which a system returns to its original state after being disturbed and in forest systems as the rate of return to mean community productivity following a disturbance. Drought events were treated as external perturbations using climate records and drought years were identified including 1948 1960 to 1962 1988 to 1989 1992 1995. Drought resilience was quantified using relative productivity deviation comparing postdrought productivity to a 10 year predrought average and the drought minimum which operationalizes return rate rather than time. The target variable used in resilience calculations was community productivity which the paper states is their measure of ecosystem function. The work is based on field sampling of 40 stands in the Desolation Wilderness and three stands in the Babbitt Peak Research Natural Area with tree cores collected in 2002 and 2003 and chronologies from 1940 to 2004. The first author affiliation is University of California Davis in the United States and the field sites are in California United States. The habitat is upper montane conifer forest stands. The disturbance is unmanipulated climatic drought and it is analyzed as discrete drought events with postdrought recovery.

The paper defines forest resilience as the ability to maintain state and functions after a perturbation and it quantifies components of resilience using Lloret et al. 2011 indices resistance recovery resilience and relative resilience. Resilience Rs is calculated as CSAI3-year post-drought divided by CSAI3-year pre-drought which is a comparison of post versus pre drought growth. The target variable used in these indices is cross-sectional area increment derived from tree ring series in stems and coarse roots. Drought events are identified from SPEI with 2005 and 2012 selected as drought events and the study compares before during and after these events. The disturbance is therefore climatic drought and it is not experimentally imposed. The drought events are treated as discrete events with three years before and three years after each event. The temporal series used for analysis is from 2000 to 2018. The study is based on field sampling of 48 trees with stem and root cores. The first author affiliation is in Madrid Spain. The study area is Alto Tajo Natural Park and the climate station location given is Molina de Aragón N40 50 40 W1 53 07. The study organism is Juniperus thurifera a tree species.

The study uses a sediment record time series from Blanca Chica Lake covering the last 250 years and analyzes temporal dynamics of multiple state variables. Resilience is quantified using early warning resilience indicators described as standard deviation and autocorrelation at lag-1 estimated on ordination scores. Autocorrelation at lag-1 is treated as a resilience indicator and is a recovery rate type metric under the provided definitions. The paper does not define a formula framework that decomposes resilience into components and instead discusses resilience indicators alongside multimodality and regime shifts. The resilience indicators are calculated from ordination scores derived from geochemical indicators and photosynthetic pigments and diatoms and cladocera and rotifera proxies which are used to infer ecosystem scale regime shifts. Pigments are described as indicating total primary productivity and primary producer composition and are ecosystem function type variables when used as productivity proxies. The analysis is based on field collected sediment core data and satellite images and gridded climate data rather than an indoor or manipulative experiment. The first author affiliation is Universidad Nacional de Mar del Plata CONICET in Argentina. The study site is explicitly Blanca Chica Lake in central Argentina with coordinates 36 degrees 50 minutes 00.9 seconds S and 60 degrees 28 minutes 00.9 seconds W. The lake is described as part of a large area of wetlands in the Pampa plain supporting the Wetland habitat classification. The discussion attributes the first transition to increased precipitation and the second to land use change and eutrophication which are treated as external drivers without experimental manipulation and therefore unmanipulated climatic and unmanipulated land use disturbances. The drivers are described as long term changes such as precipitation increase since 1870 and twentieth century land use change and nutrient input indicating a press type disturbance framing. The record spans about 250 years which corresponds to 91250 days between first and last measurements.

The study used long term biomonitoring with 2500 aquatic invertebrate community samples from 179 sites in 1990–2022 and matched each sample to hydrological drought metrics including low flow anomaly and time since drought defined as days since the most recent hydrological drought. Recovery was analysed directly as change over time since drought because type ii recovery models assessed community change over time post drought and the discussion states post drought recovery rates. The introduction presents resistance and resilience together as resistance and or resilience and the aims state modulate invertebrate community resistance and resilience to drought. Resilience quantification is based on trajectories with time since drought and statements such as WHPT ASPT recovered faster and increases in richness were slower or higher which indicates recovery rate rather than recovery time. The variables used to represent community responses and recovery include taxa richness and deviation from the average community composition and WHPT ASPT and the question requires target variable types used to quantify resilience so composition and diversity apply. The biological organisation level is community because metrics summarize invertebrate community responses. The approach includes field observation because samples were collected in rivers and also modeling and simulation because drought and flow alteration variables came from coupled groundwater and surface water hydrological models with simulated discharge. The first author affiliation is Nottingham Trent University in the UK. The study area is rivers fed by England's chalk aquifers so the field country is the UK. No site coordinates are provided so the location is approximated from Nottingham in England and the range is set to 0 because distinct coordinates are not given. The habitat is freshwater rivers which fits wetland in the provided habitat options. The focal organisms are aquatic invertebrates so the taxon is Animalia. Drought is treated as hydrological drought defined from low flow anomalies and analysed as an unmanipulated climatic extreme event and the discussion describes drought as pulsed.

The introduction defines resilience as the combination of resistance and recovery and defines resistance as the ability to maintain its state by withstanding disturbance and recovery as the ability to rapidly go back to pre-disturbance conditions. The study states it investigates drivers of bull kelp forest resistance and later states it evaluated ecological resistance by measuring patterns of persistence cover and abundance. Bull kelp cover and persistence were calculated from a time series of PlanetScope satellite data from 2016 to 2022 and bull kelp abundance and cover were also measured in situ by SCUBA transects in 2020 to 2022. Because resilience related metrics are derived from observed bull kelp cover persistence and abundance the measurement type is observed. The quantification is resistance because the paper explicitly frames the analysis as ecological resistance and uses persistence cover and abundance as resistance traits rather than measuring return time or recovery rate after a defined disturbance. The target variables used to quantify resistance are bull kelp cover and bull kelp abundance and persistence is derived from canopy presence in the satellite cover time series. These variables describe a single species population level response rather than individual level or community level indices. The approach is field observation because it uses in situ field surveys and remote sensing observations and the lab experiment is used to test a mechanism rather than to quantify resistance itself. The first author primary affiliation is Institut de Ciències del Mar in Barcelona Spain so the institution country is ES. The field data are from northern California USA and specific coordinates are provided for Bodega Marine Laboratory and Doran Beach and the study sites are in northern California USA. A representative coordinate from the methods is the Bodega Marine Laboratory location 38.301 N 123.667 W and the study region is nearshore coastal kelp habitat which fits Marine Neritic. The taxa involved in the resistance quantification are bull kelp Nereocystis luetkeana and purple sea urchin Strongylocentrotus purpuratus. The disturbance context described for the decline includes a marine heatwave and loss of predators with urchin proliferation and these are treated as unmanipulated events in the observational analysis. The heatwave is described as occurring during 2014 to 2016 which is a discrete event so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The satellite observation window used for persistence spans 2016 to 2022 which is 7 years or 2555 days.

The study proposes a framework to assess ecological network resilience and states that it evaluates resilience from the perspectives of robustness and redundancy and defines resilience as balancing robustness and redundancy with equations using robustness and redundancy indicators. Resilience is quantified from network structure indicators including agglomeration connectivity network density diversity transformation and complexity and then combined into robustness redundancy and resilience rather than from observed recovery trajectories of an ecosystem state variable. Although NDVI and other spatial datasets are used they are used to build indices and resistance surfaces and to construct the ecological network rather than to measure recovery dynamics after a real disturbance. The shock simulation removes nodes and edges and recalculates network resilience which is a modelled structural failure scenario rather than observed disturbance response. The approach therefore includes field observation via remote sensing and spatial datasets and modeling and simulation via circuit theory linkage mapper and shock simulation. The first author affiliation is Guangdong University of Technology in Guangzhou PR China. The study areas are three urban agglomerations in China namely Yangtze River Delta Beijing Tianjin Hebei and Pearl River Delta. No coordinates are provided so an average location in eastern China is reported to represent the three regions and the range reflects their broad separation. The focus is urban agglomerations and urban ecological networks so the dominant habitat type is urban. Because the level is landscape and resilience is for an ecological network rather than a specific organism taxon is not applicable. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

The introduction defines resilience as the rate of recovery from disturbance. The methods describe a manipulative field experiment in 12 tidal marshes where aboveground vegetation was removed by applying a systemic herbicide glyphosate and clipping new shoots through March 2010. Recovery was tracked using repeated measurements in October from 2010 to 2020 of vegetation cover vegetation height and plant community composition in disturbed plots compared with control plots. Recovery time was determined for each metric and relative recovery rate was calculated as 1 divided by recovery time in months with a modified calculation for the site that did not completely recover. Because resilience is quantified from observed recovery dynamics after an imposed disturbance the measurement type is observed and the quantification is recovery rate. The target variables used to quantify resilience are vegetation cover plant height and community composition which correspond to cover growth and composition. The variables are measured across plant communities within plots and include multiple species so the level is community. The study is a field experiment because disturbances were created in situ in coastal marshes. The first author affiliation includes Tianjin Normal University in China so the institution country is CN. The study sites are on the coast of Georgia USA so the field country is US and the habitat type is wetland. The dominant organisms measured are marsh plants so the taxon is Plantae. The disturbance includes chemical application of glyphosate and structural removal of vegetation by clipping so the disturbance types are manipulated chemical and manipulated structural. The disturbance is treated as a discrete imposed event followed by recovery monitoring so the pattern is pulse. The observation window for resilience spans from the first post disturbance measurements in October 2010 to the last measurements in October 2020 which is 11 years or 4015 days.

The study quantified spatiotemporal patterns in mangrove range expansion and contraction using coastal wetland coverage data for 1978 1993 2000 and 2011 and related those changes to extreme freeze events. Resilience is explicitly defined as the capacity to recover after being affected by a freeze event and resistance as the capacity to withstand a freeze event and they are discussed together without a higher level variable. Recovery was evaluated as increases in mangrove area after the 1980s freeze events and as percentage change in mangrove forest area between 1993 to 2000 and 2000 to 2011 when temperatures were above the threshold zone. The target variable used for these resilience statements is mangrove coverage area and mangrove forest dominance derived from mangrove area within cells. The focal organism is black mangrove Avicennia germinans. The disturbance is extreme winter freeze events based on air temperatures and specific freeze dates and years and these are unmanipulated climatic events. The disturbance is treated as discrete events with specific dates and years. The wetland study polygon spans Golden Meadow about 29.35 N 90.25 W to Port Fourchon about 29.10 N 90.20 W in Louisiana USA and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation is U S Geological Survey in Lafayette Louisiana USA. The observation window for coverage change spans from October 1978 to October 2011.

The study states that it uses remotely sensed greenness and that it quantifies resilience using temporal autocorrelation of EVI during 2000 to 2019. It defines vegetation resilience as the recovery rate from deviations caused by perturbations to the equilibrium state. It estimates time varying lag 1 autocorrelation of EVI with a 16 day interval using a Bayesian dynamic linear model and uses the posterior mean of negative lag 1 autocorrelation of EVI as a proxy of vegetation resilience because high autocorrelation indicates low resilience. This is a direct time series based quantification from the system state variable EVI so the measurement is observed. The target variable used to quantify resilience is EVI greenness which the paper links to vegetation productivity and photosynthetic capacity and it is treated as an ecosystem scale state variable. The analysis domain is the NASA ABoVE core domain mainly Alaska and northwestern Canada and the Methods state the region spans over Alaska and northwestern Canada. Example coordinates are provided in Extended Data Fig 2 for pixels in Northwest Territories Alaska and British Columbia and the average of those three points is N62.1 W140.1 with the farthest point differing by 33.0 degrees when summing maximum latitude and longitude differences. The first author affiliation is The Ohio State University in the United States. Disturbances analysed include climate driven land cover change and fire disturbance and the text also discusses warming drought and moisture stress as climate stresses. Fires and land cover changes are treated as abrupt changes with analysis of 5 years before and 5 years after which is pulse while climate stress and warming are treated as ongoing press disturbances. The EVI record used is MODIS 16 day data from 2000 to 2019 which is about 7300 days.

The study defines ecosystem health using the vitality organization resilience paradigm and gives the equation EHI = W1×V + W2×O + W3×R. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics but is assigned using land resilience coefficients and modified using NDVI with Ri = NDVIi/NDVImeanj × rj which is a proxy construction. Table 1 labels NDVI 2020 as resilience measurement and land use 2020 as contribution and organization measurement and resistance surface construction showing resilience is derived from static spatial layers rather than observed recovery after disturbance. No disturbance event is imposed or tracked for recovery and the work focuses on ecological security and ecological network construction and reconstruction. The approach uses remote sensing and GIS tools including Landsat 8 MODIS and ArcGIS and FRAGSTATS and Linkage Mapper and Circuitscape which are field observation data sources. The first author affiliation is Xinjiang University Urumqi China. The study area is Tacheng-Emin Basin northwest China with coordinates 44•55′-47•15′ N and 82•15′–85•23′ E and the average coordinate is reported. The results state grasslands contribute 78.21 percent of ecosystem service value and grassland is the main component of ecological sources indicating grassland is the dominant habitat for the resilience related mapping. Because the level of analysis is based on land use and landscape indices and resistance surfaces the organizational level is landscape and taxon is not applicable. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

The study frames resilience as a restoration related concept but it does not quantify resilience from recovery dynamics and instead evaluates restoration potential and resilience relevance using soil seed bank and vegetation indicators. The abstract states close to nature restoration is recognized for promoting ecosystem resilience and the study investigates variation of seed banks and aboveground plant communities along an anthropogenic disturbance gradient. The discussion states the persistent seed bank abundance serves as a vital indicator of vegetation resilience and also states the soil seed bank had high degrees of stability and resilience. The measured variables used in the core analyses are seed bank density and richness and aboveground vegetation abundance and coverage with comparisons across disturbance gradients and path models linking disturbance to these indicators. No time series of system recovery after a defined disturbance end is used to quantify resilience and the disturbance gradient is spatial not a before after recovery design. The work is based on field surveys and soil sampling in Wuhai City Inner Mongolia in northwestern China at the Xinxing coal mine and a local natural protected area. Figure 1 locates the study area in Wuhai and the text specifies Wuhai City Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region China. The dominant habitat is described as arid desert with desert as the main vegetation type. The focal biota are plants because aboveground plant communities and soil seed banks are surveyed and germinated. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable under the instructions.

The study states resilience is the ability to absorb disturbances while maintaining structure and function and it evaluates community resilience by quantifying resistance and stability. The Methods state species percent cover was recorded on permanent transects multiple times from 1992 to 2017 and analyses used Bray Curtis dissimilarity and NMDS ordination based on vegetation composition. Resistance was quantified as the distance between a transects initial position and its position at successive monitoring points and stability was quantified as the average distance moved between consecutive monitoring events. These are direct calculations from observed community data rather than proxies. The disturbance is repeated wildfires described as wildfires and fire history was derived from GIS layers and used to build a fire index. Fires are treated as discrete events in a timeline with surveys before and after fires. The field sites are in and around the Arid Lands Ecology Reserve and the Columbia Basin in Washington USA. The first author affiliation is The Ohio State University in the United States. The habitat is described as sagebrush steppe a semi arid shrubland ecosystem. The observation window spans from 1992 to 2017 which is 25 years.

The study used four repeated inventories of twelve permanent sampling plots in years 1 10 20 and 30 and calculated an index of resilience from the system trajectory. Resilience was defined as the basal area ratio for the eighth over the second inventory cycle as an index of resilience against fluctuations due to natural self thinning and disease disturbances such as epidemic insect infestations in the course of thirty years. This is an observed measurement because it uses repeated measurements of live tree DBH to calculate stand basal area over time and then computes the final over initial basal area ratio. The quantification matches recovery degree because resilience R is the final over initial stand basal area ratio and the discussion states basal area losses due to fir decline were recovered by hardwoods. The target variable used to quantify resilience is stand basal area which the methods describe as indicative of species dominance forest cover and biomass yield so it is coded as biomass. The level is ecosystem because stand basal area is a stand level functional property irrespective of species identity when used as total basal area ratio for the stand. The approach is field observation because the data come from permanent sampling plots in the provincial forest inventory that were re measured in 5 year cycles. The first author affiliation is Cape Breton University in Canada. The field data are from Eastern Cape Breton Island Nova Scotia Canada. Exact plot coordinates are proprietary and not provided so location and location range are NA. The habitat is forest because the study focuses on cool humid forests and permanent forest sampling plots. The taxon is Plantae because the subjects are tree species. Disturbances considered for resilience include epidemic insect infestations and natural self thinning which are unmanipulated biotic and unmanipulated structural disturbances. The disturbance is treated as press because resilience is assessed against fluctuations over the 30 year period without a defined single event recovery window. Observation duration is thirty years which is 10950 days.

Framework: stability = resistance + resilience + recovery + temporal stability
Justification: The study quantified resilience using observed ANPP data across years that bracketed a natural extreme snowfall reduction event described as persistently low snow depth from October 2018 to April 2019. The introduction defines a multidimensional stability framework stating that resistance resilience recovery and temporal stability are pivotal dimensions contributing to stability. The methods define resilience as the rate at which an ecosystem returns to its pre disturbance status and quantify it as ln ANPP2020 divided by ANPP2019. ANPP is measured in the field using exclosure cages and biomass harvests with sampling dates spanning 2018 to 2020. The target variable used in the resilience formula is ANPP which is an ecosystem productivity measure. The study area is the HulunBuir Grassland Agro Ecosystem Station at 49 degrees 28 minutes north and 120 degrees 10 minutes east in Inner Mongolia northeastern China. The first author affiliation is China Agricultural University in Beijing China. The disturbance is an unmanipulated climatic extreme snowfall reduction event and it is treated as a discrete event with pre event mid event and post event years. The observation window for resilience spans from 2018 to 2020 which is two years or 730 days.

The study uses time series remote sensing observations from 2001 to 2021 including NDVI EVI and SIF to detect disturbance events and quantify resistance resilience and regime shift rate. Resilience is explicitly defined as the rate of return of an ecosystem toward its pre disturbed state following a disturbance and is calculated as a resilience rate R’ = M’ divided by ΔT’ in Table 2. The analysis framework states ecosystem stability is evaluated using indices resistance resilience and regime shift rate and the introduction states resistance and resilience are key indicators of ecosystem stability. The target variables used to quantify resilience are vegetation indices and SIF which the paper treats as vegetation indicators linked to photosynthesis and vegetation growth state making them ecosystem function proxies. The study area is the Minjiang river basin located between 28.25 to 33.6667 N and 99.6167 to 104.6167 E and the abrupt disturbance analysis focuses on Wenchuan County as the epicenter area. The first author affiliation is Xi’an University of Science and Technology in China. Disturbances analyzed include a winter spring drought from 2011 to 2012 and the May 2008 Wenchuan earthquake and both are treated as discrete events with recovery assessed afterward such as vegetation recovered to near normal levels by 2010. The observation window for the main time series is 2001.01 to 2021.12 which is 21 years or 7670 days using 365 days per year.

The study states that it uses land use data from 2005 to 2020 and constructs a landscape ecological network using the Minimum Cumulative Resistance model and complex network methods and it evaluates optimization by comparing connectivity and robustness before and after optimization. Resilience is not quantified from a system recovery trajectory but is discussed as a network property because the paper reports that the optimized structure exhibits stronger connectivity robustness and resilience and that robustness refers to the ability of a network to maintain its original functionality under damaged conditions. The robustness analysis is based on simulation experiments using random and malicious attack scenarios rather than observed post disturbance recovery of an ecological state variable. Therefore resilience is inferred from network robustness metrics rather than directly observed recovery. The framework is none because the paper does not define an explicit equation including resilience and instead discusses stability and robustness and resilience without an explicit additive framework. The target variable used to represent resilience is network robustness and connectivity which are complex network properties. The level is landscape because the analysis concerns ecological patches corridors and a city scale ecological spatial network. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study constructs models and runs attack simulations on the network. The first author affiliation is Beijing Forestry University in Beijing China. The field data are from Linfen City Shanxi Province China with coordinates given as 35.23N to 36.57N and 110.22E to 112.34E and the average is reported. Habitat is multi because the MSPA foreground includes forests grasslands and water bodies and the study also includes croplands and urban built up areas in optimization.

The study quantified resistance using individual tree survival following wildfire events and relative bark thickness and post fire juvenile height growth as indicators of fire resistance. The study quantified resilience using seed cone presence at the time of field sampling and the frequency of Pinus ponderosa seed cone years from 2006 to 2017 as indicators of resilience to wildfire. These variables are measured from field plots in 15 study sites within and adjacent to recent 1988 to 2010 wildfires in the southern Rocky Mountains USA. The introduction explicitly pairs the concepts as resistance meaning individual survival and resilience meaning population persistence following fire and Figure 1 describes traits promoting fire resistance and resilience. Resilience is not quantified as recovery time or recovery rate of an ecosystem state variable but via reproductive maturity thresholds and cone production frequency so the quantification category is other. The approach is field observation because the methods describe establishing field plots and reconstructing survival and measuring traits and vital rates. The first author affiliation is University of Colorado Boulder in the United States. The disturbance is wildfire and it is unmanipulated because the study uses recent wildfires rather than imposed burns. The disturbance is treated as a pulse because it refers to discrete wildfire events between 1988 and 2010 and then post fire measurements and reconstructions. The observation duration is approximated as 30 years from 1988 to 2018 because burned plots were restricted to areas that burned once between 1984 and the time of field surveys 2016 to 2018 and the fires were 1988 to 2010. The habitat is forest because the study concerns conifer forests in the southern Rocky Mountains. The taxon is Plantae because the focal organisms are Pinus ponderosa and Pseudotsuga menziesii. No explicit coordinates are provided in the text so location and location range are NA.

Quantification: Recovery rate; Recovery time; Recovery degree
Justification: The study quantified resilience using tree ring proxy data and standard tree ring width index chronologies across 54 sites in the Tianshan Mountains. Extreme drought years were identified from SPEI and growth depression events in 1974 and 1997 were selected as pointer years. Growth response was evaluated using resistance RT recovery RC and resilience RS following Lloret et al 2011. Additional indices quantified recovery trajectory including recovery period RE defined as duration until reaching pre drought growth level and average recovery rate ARR defined as growth reduction divided by RE and total growth reduction TGR. These indices are computed from observed tree ring width index before during and after drought which is direct system response. The paper explicitly treats resilience as composed of resistance and recovery by calculating RT RC and RS as resilience components. The disturbance is climatic drought and it is not experimentally manipulated. The discussion classifies 1974 as press droughts and 1997 as pulse droughts. The first author affiliation is in Lanzhou China and the study sites are in the Tianshan Mountains northwestern China. The focal organisms are Schrenk spruce and Siberian larch which are plants in forest habitat. The observation window for identifying drought and growth series is since 1960 and includes 1960 to 2008 which is 49 years or 17885 days but the resilience comparison focuses on three year averages before and after each drought and recovery period with mean RE values around 3 to 4 years so a conservative duration for the resilience observation around each event is 8 years or 2920 days.

The study used a freshwater mesocosm experiment and quantified stability properties from measured community data. Resilience was defined as engineering resilience and measured as the slope of the log response ratio of abundance versus time and as the slope of Bray Curtis similarity versus time. This is a direct quantification of recovery speed after pesticide perturbation so the measurement type is observed and the quantification is recovery rate. The framework is explicitly given as stability requiring resistance resilience recovery and invariability. Functional stability used total abundance and compositional stability used Bray Curtis similarity of community composition so the target variable types are abundance and composition at the community level. The experiment was performed outdoors at IMDEA Water Institute in Alcala de Henares Madrid Spain so the institution country and field country are Spain and the approach is field experiment. The location is Alcala de Henares Madrid Spain which corresponds to approximately N40.48 W3.36 and the study uses one site so the location range is 0. The system is a freshwater mesocosm filled with sediments and water from an artificial lagoon and stocked with aquatic communities so the habitat type is wetland. Organism groups included phytoplankton zooplankton macroinvertebrates and macrophytes so taxa include Chromista Animalia and Plantae. Disturbances were manipulated applications of insecticide and herbicide and a press continuous nutrient addition so disturbance types are manipulated chemical and manipulated resource with pulse and press patterns. Resistance was measured at day 15 and recovery at day 50 and sampling occurred before and after pesticide application so the observation window for disturbance response spans 50 days.

The introduction states that the study focuses on response to drought resistance and recovery of photosynthesis after drought resilience. The methods describe a mesocosm experiment where water table was lowered to 30 cm below the surface for 43 days and then readjusted back to 10 cm below the surface for a three week recovery period. Photosynthesis was measured repeatedly as maximum photosynthesis Pmax before drought after 6 weeks of drought and after 1 2 and 3 weeks of recovery. Resistance recovery and resilience were calculated from Pmax using formulas with Pmax pre Pmax end and Pmax rec. Resilience is quantified as the drop in Pmax from pre drought to end of the recovery period as a percent of pre drought Pmax which is a recovery degree measure. The target variable used for resilience quantification is photosynthesis Pmax which is a physiological indicator. The study subjects are Sphagnum mosses and the paper refers to them as mosses and species of Sphagnum which are plants. The mesocosms were collected from Lakkasuo peatland complex in Southern Finland at 61 47 N 24 18 E. The first author affiliation is University of Eastern Finland in Joensuu Finland. The habitat is peatland and fen and bog which are wetlands. The disturbance is experimentally imposed water table lowering described as ecohydrological drought and periodic drought. The drought is applied for a defined 43 day period followed by rewetting so it is treated as a pulse. The timeline shows acclimatization 7 weeks drought 43 days and recovery 3 weeks giving 86 days from drought start to end of recovery.

Target variable type: Land use; Ecosystem function; Regional network parameter
Justification: The introduction defines ecological resiliency as the ability to resist severe stress and restore their original state. The study does not quantify recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable after a defined disturbance and instead builds an overall resiliency evaluation system using entropy weight in the TOPSIS model. The methods state that four ecological resiliency factors and sub indicators were used namely land use and cover change normalized difference vegetation index riparian buffers and landscape pattern index. These are indicators and proxies derived from remote sensing and landscape metrics rather than direct measurement of system recovery after disturbance. The discussion states that the indexes were chosen based on assumption that natural area with better integrity have better resiliency while urban development and areas disturbed by human beings have reduced ecological resiliency. The approach includes field observation because it uses Landsat remote sensing images and DEM for 1995 2005 2010 2015 2018 and 2022. The approach also includes modeling and simulation because it uses the CA MC model to predict and map ecological resiliency change for 2026 and 2030. The first author affiliation is Yunnan Agricultural University Kunming China. The study area is Dianchi Lake Basin in southwest China with coordinates given as 24.28 to 25.28 N and 102.30 to 103.00 E and the average is N24.88 E102.75. The habitat context includes land use types such as construction land cultivated land grassland forest and water area so multiple habitat types are explicitly included. Because the level of analysis is an integrated basin wide land and vegetation and landscape pattern evaluation it corresponds to landscape level. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration for disturbance response are not specified for resilience quantification.

The introduction states that the experiment experienced marsh die off and that this allowed the authors to explore marsh resilience as the capacity to maintain living Spartina cover in the face of die off. The methods define a die off severity score based on percent bare ground and state that this provides an indication of saltmarsh resilience to die off under different grazing treatments and that it does not distinguish resistance and recovery. The methods also state that they simulated hog damage and measured recovery as the natural log transformed response ratio of vegetation metrics in hog disturbed plots relative to undisturbed reference plots at 6 and 12 months. These resilience metrics use observed vegetation state variables rather than proxies. The resilience related target variables are living plant cover and vegetation biomass and stem height is also used but the target variable type options include cover and biomass. The study uses a replicated 51 month horse exclusion field experiment and a broad scale observational survey of marshes along the US coast. The first author affiliation is University of Maryland in the United States. The experimental site is Cumberland Island Georgia at 30.85 N 81.45 W and the observational survey spans 30.52 N Florida to 38.30 N Maryland so the average coordinate is reported. The discussion describes resilience facets as die off resistance and recovery from simulated hog damage. The die off is linked to drought and described as associated with drought and there was a period of severe drought. The hog disturbance is simulated by uprooting Spartina clumps and trampling to simulate wallowing and rooting. The observation window for the experiment is March 2017 to June 2021 which is 51 months.

The study goal was to enhance resilience and it assessed ecosystem attributes from Ecopath models rather than measuring recovery of a single state variable through a direct resilience metric. The methods state that the authors constructed Ecopath models and then assessed ecosystem attributes including connectance index system omnivory index Finn cycling index Finn mean path length transfer efficiency and Shannon diversity index. The discussion states that resilience increased in the long term while referring to better performance of ecosystem attributes such as total system throughput total primary production to total respiration and Shannon diversity index. Because resilience is concluded from changes in these indicators rather than directly quantified as recovery time recovery rate recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability from a defined disturbance response trajectory the measurement is inferred. The methods explicitly frame assessment as vitality stability and maturity of the Ecopath models. The data were collected in the Chichiawan Stream in central Taiwan with monitoring sites at N24 22 15 0 E121 18 38 0 and N24 21 16 1 E121 18 49 8. The first author affiliation is National Chung Hsing University in Taichung Taiwan. The approach is field observation because biomasses and other parameters were obtained from field surveys and monitoring. The habitat is a stream in a national park so it is coded as wetland. Taxon is NA because resilience was quantified at the ecosystem level using food web and ecosystem attributes rather than a single organism group. Disturbance fields are NA because the measurement is inferred under the provided rules.

Framework: ecological stability = resistance + resilience + recovery + temporal stability
Justification: The introduction defines ecological stability as containing resistance resilience recovery and temporal stability and defines resilience as the rate at which a function returns to its pre disturbance status. The methods quantify resilience as ANPP2019 divided by ANPP2018 using field measured above ground net primary productivity from 2017 to 2019. The flooding event is identified from precipitation data with 2018 exceeding the 95th percentile and 2017 and 2019 within the normal range and the study uses this event to quantify resistance resilience and recovery. Because resilience is computed from observed ANPP before during and after the flooding year the measurement is observed and the quantification corresponds to recovery rate. The target variable used in the resilience formula is ANPP which is above ground net primary productivity and is treated as plant community productivity. The study is a nitrogen addition field experiment with plots receiving six nitrogen addition levels since 2011 and plant biomass harvested in field quadrats. The site is on the meadow steppe of Ulan Buton Inner Mongolia China with coordinates 42 degrees 32 minutes to 42 degrees 38 minutes north and 117 degrees 5 minutes to 117 degrees 8 minutes east. The first author affiliation is Peking University Beijing China. The disturbance is an extreme flooding event caused by heavy precipitation during the growing season and is treated as a discrete event year with pre flooding and post flooding years.

The study used repeated annual inventories from 2015 to 2021 where individual tree stem diameters were measured and basal area increment was calculated and then aggregated as mean basal area increment per plot community. Drought resistance was quantified as the ratio between tree growth during drought and tree growth during the respective pre drought period. Resilience was defined as the capacity to reach pre disturbance performance levels and was estimated as the ratio between post drought growth and pre drought growth and the authors state they refer to this index as early resilience. These ratios use observed growth before during and after the 2018 to 2020 drought and therefore resilience measurement is based on direct observational system response. The paper presents resistance and resilience together as concepts used to quantify growth responses to drought without defining a higher level variable that includes them. The target variable used in the resistance and resilience indices is basal area increment which is a growth measure. The resilience quantification corresponds to recovery degree because it compares post drought growth to pre drought growth and indicates whether post drought growth is comparable to pre drought growth. The resistance quantification corresponds to resistance because it compares drought period growth to pre drought growth. The work is a tree diversity experiment at the MyDiv Experiment with manipulated tree species richness and mycorrhizal type in field plots. The first author affiliation is in Leipzig Germany. The study site is close to Halle Saale in Germany with coordinates 51 degrees 23 minutes north and 11 degrees 53 minutes east. The site was formerly used agriculturally until 2012 and as grassland until establishment in 2015 so the habitat origin described for the experimental site is agricultural. The disturbance is the 2018 to 2020 drought identified using SPEI and soil moisture and it is not experimentally imposed so it is unmanipulated climatic. The drought is treated as three consecutive drought years 2018 to 2020 with no defined end until after 2020 so it is a press disturbance within the study window. The observation window for growth measurements spans 2015 to 2021 which is seven consecutive years corresponding to 2190 days using 365 days per year.

The introduction defines resilience as the capacity for populations to resist and recover from disturbances and later states that they quantify resistance compensation and recovery as resilience attributes. The methods state that they extracted matrix population models describing wild unmanipulated populations and quantified demographic resilience using transient metrics. The methods define disturbance as an external discrete event that modifies the structural composition of a population and they calculate transient metrics describing responses following disturbance relative to equilibrium. The quantified resilience metrics are first step attenuation and maximal attenuation for resistance reactivity and maximal amplification for compensation and damping ratio and period of oscillation for recovery. These metrics are derived from demographic matrix models and therefore the target variable is demographic parameters at the population level. The disturbance is not an observed event in the field but a demographic perturbation to population structure used to compute transient responses so it is model structural and treated as a discrete event. The study uses demographic databases and climate time series linked by latitude and longitude but the provided text does not report specific field countries or coordinates. The methods include terrestrial and freshwater populations across many species and exclude marine populations indicating multiple habitat types overall. The first author affiliation is University of St Andrews in the United Kingdom.

The study quantified resilience using common garden stress treatments where algae were exposed to heat shock or desiccation and then measured recovery of PSII maximum quantum yield Fv/Fm after a defined recovery period. The Methods state that physiological resilience to heat shock was determined by exposing acclimated algae to heat shock for 3 h at multiple temperatures and that after a 24 h recovery photoinhibition of PSII maximum quantum yield Fv/Fm was measured. The Methods also state that physiological resilience to desiccation was tested by desiccating tips for 90 120 and 180 min and measuring Fv/Fm prior to desiccation and again following a 20 h recovery. The Discussion repeatedly frames resilience as the capacity to recover Fv/Fm to pre stress levels and reports that marginal populations had reduced capacity to recover. Because resilience is quantified from the system response after imposed stress it is observed rather than inferred. No explicit equation style framework defining resilience as a component of another variable is provided in the Introduction Methods or Discussion. The target variable used for resilience quantification is Fv/Fm which is a physiological indicator. The measurements are on apical tips from individuals with replicate tips from individuals per treatment indicating an individual level response. The approach includes field observation because the study measured intertidal thermal environment with in situ dataloggers and also includes indoor experiment because stress treatments and recovery were conducted in laboratory tanks and water baths. The first author affiliation is CCMAR CIMAR University of Algarve Faro Portugal indicating PT. Field sampling occurred in southwest UK and northern Portugal with sites including Widemouth Bay Cornwall and Viana do Castelo and Praia de Amorosa. Coordinates are provided for Viana do Castelo and Praia de Amorosa and UK sites are named but not given coordinates so the location is represented by the average of the provided Portugal coordinates and an approximate UK site coordinate. The habitat is intertidal rocky shore described as intertidal habitats and rocky shores which fits Coastal. The organisms are intertidal seaweed Fucus serratus and Fucus vesiculosus which are brown algae and thus Chromista. The disturbance is manipulated heat shock and desiccation treatments which are abiotic climatic stressors applied as discrete events so the pattern is pulse. The observation window for resilience measurement spans from the pre stress measurement through the recovery measurement which is 24 h for heat shock and 20 h for desiccation so a duration of about 2 days captures the resilience observation period.

The study uses time series of vegetation dynamics anomalies fraction of absorbed photosynthetically active radiation absorbed by vegetation FAPAR between 1982 and 2011 and time series of a drought indicator SPEI03. It states that stability of vegetation to drought is measured via two metrics resistance and resilience. It defines resistance as the direct impact of drought on the vegetation and defines ecosystem resilience as the speed of recovery to the equilibrium state after a disturbance event. It maps resilience using an autoregressive FAPAR term and states that high autocorrelation values were suggested to be indicative of slow recovery from drought. The target variable used in the resilience model is FAPAR anomalies described as an indicator for monthly plant productivity. The approach combines remote sensing field observation data with regression distributed lag models described as models developed in the methods. The first author affiliation is European Environment Agency Copenhagen Denmark. SPEI is described as a global monthly gridded dataset and the location is given as continental Europe but the drought dataset is global so the field country is coded as Global. The disturbance is drought described as an extreme climatic event and analysed without experimental manipulation. Drought is described as developing over months or years and the analysis uses negative SPEI anomalies across the growing season over 1982 to 2011 indicating a press type driver. The observation window spans 1982 to 2011 which is 30 years.

The study imposed plateau pika density and mowing intensity as experimental treatments in a two factor randomized block design with 27 plots and maintained pika density by capturing and resetting pikas each August and applied mowing in late July and mid September each year. It states that four vegetation indices were used to measure the recovery rate of various patches and it defines resilience measurement by calculating RLDC and RSDC from nadir viewing drone images. RLDC and RSDC are ratios based on patch coverage derived from UAV image classification so resilience is quantified from observed plot data rather than only proxies. The introduction states objectives include exploring the interference threshold that different patches can withstand to maintain the resilience under different levels of disturbance which corresponds to a threshold based latitude concept. Recovery rate is explicitly quantified using vegetation indices from hyperspectral imagery taken in June and August 2021. The resilience ratios use patch coverage from UAV imagery taken on August 6 of 2021. The target variables used for resilience quantification are RLDC and RSDC which are coverage ratios and the recovery rate indices are vegetation indices described as related to chlorophyll content photosynthetic efficiency anthocyanin content and senescence which are ecosystem function proxies. The study site coordinates are given as 34 41 07 N 101 46 02 E in Qinghai Province China and the first author affiliation is Qinghai University in China. The habitat is described as a typical grassland type of alpine meadow. The disturbances are plateau pika activity and simulated grazing by mowing which are biotic disturbance and biological resource use respectively and they are maintained across years so the disturbance is treated as press. The plots were completed in July 2018 and tested for 3 years by the time of in situ sampling in 2021 which gives an observation duration of 3 years.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the ability of ecosystems to maintain their functions in order to resist or recover from severe disturbances and the study interprets resilience as any buffering of the negative effects of forest loss on the provision of seed dispersal. The methods quantify seed dispersal using counts of dispersed seeds collected in sampling quadrats and analyze how seed rain distribution matches forest cover along a gradient of forest loss. This uses direct observational data on the ecosystem function seed dispersal rather than a proxy index. The quantification matches resistance because resilience is operationalized as buffering of negative effects of habitat loss on seed dispersal rather than as a return rate time or degree after a discrete event. The disturbance is habitat loss described as an anthropogenic disturbance gradient of forest loss and fragmentation and it is not experimentally manipulated. The disturbance is treated as a persistent landscape condition across the study period so it is press. The study system is located in Sierra de Peña Mayor Asturias Province Spain with coordinates 43°18′00″N 5°30′29″W. The first author affiliation is University of Oviedo Oviedo Spain. The observation spans three consecutive periods from 2007 to 2009 described as three years so the duration is 1095 days. The habitat is temperate secondary forest of the Cantabrian range.

The study states that stability encompasses variability resistance and resilience and it presents stability as a multi-faceted property encompassing variability resistance and resilience to perturbations. The Methods define resilience as the rate slope of recovery from the minimum calculated by the slope of a linear regression between the response variable and days post disturbance with loge transformation of days. The study directly measured responses immediately prior to disturbance and at 0.5 5 10 20 and 40 d post disturbance in treatment reaches and also measured control reaches prior to and 40 d following the perturbation. The disturbance was experimentally imposed by simulated bed movement using a jet of high pressure water and is described as bed scouring disturbance consistent with bed moving floods. The response variables used for stability components include invertebrate abundance taxon richness and multivariate community composition based on Bray Curtis similarity. The study streams were in the upper Waimakariri and Rakaia River catchments of New Zealand South Island. The first author affiliation is the University of Maine in the United States. The streams are described as grassland streams in unmodified mixed shrub and tussock grasslands. The observation window for post disturbance recovery measurements ends at 40 d post disturbance.

The study defines resilience as a system tendency to return to the original state following an initially strong displacement and defines resistance as a tendency to avoid displacement from its original undisturbed state. It tests signatures of resistance and resilience by comparing levels of genetic and phenotypic diversity before vs after floods using archived historical samples from 2004 2005 2016 and 2017. The disturbance is two extreme floods one between January and March 2005 and one in December 2016 and these are treated as discrete events bracketed by before and after sampling. Resilience is evidenced by statements that impacts on phenotypic diversity were short lived and that size went back to previous levels before the second flood and that admixture was back to previous levels before the second flood suggesting resilience. This is recovery degree because it is assessed as return to preflood levels and patterns rather than an explicit rate or time to recovery. Target variables used in these resilience claims include genetic admixture and population structure and gene diversity and allelic richness and body length and male melanic colour patterns. These variables are measured on guppy populations at sites so the level is population. Sampling and data are from the Marianne and Paria Rivers on Trinidad and fish were caught each year in February or March so the field country is Trinidad and Tobago. The first author affiliation is McGill University in Montreal Canada so the institution country is Canada. The approach is field observation because fish were captured in the same sites each year and analysed retrospectively rather than a manipulative experiment. No coordinates are provided beyond a map figure so location and range cannot be extracted. The habitat is rivers so it fits Wetland under the provided habitat categories. The observation duration spans from 2004 to 2017 which is 13 years or 4745 days using 365 days per year.

The abstract states that the study proposes adding ecosystem services abundance as a resilience descriptor to the InVEST habitat risk assessment model. The introduction defines vulnerability as a function of exposure to stressors and of sensitivity to impact and resilience. The methods describe that the modified HRA_ES-2 model adds ecosystem services abundance as a resilience attribute and assigns weights to provisioning regulation and cultural services. The resilience descriptor is therefore constructed from ecosystem services abundance rather than measured from system recovery dynamics. The methods specify that the work applies InVEST Habitat Risk Assessment models and compares HRA-1 and HRA_ES-2 using GIS and statistical tests which is a modeling approach. The study area is the Atlantic coastal region adjacent to the Mondego River mouth in mainland Portugal. The first author affiliation is University of Coimbra in Coimbra Portugal. The figure caption and study area description indicate coastal and estuarine habitats including seagrass and saltmarsh but the dominant setting is an Atlantic coastal region adjacent to an estuary. No direct time series of ecosystem state recovery is used to quantify resilience and disturbances are treated as stressors in a risk model rather than observed recovery events. No explicit observation window for resilience response is provided for a disturbance and recovery sequence.

Framework: ecological vulnerability = ecological sensitivity + ecological resilience
Justification: The study constructs an ecological vulnerability assessment framework founded on resilience-sensitivity and computes an ecological vulnerability index using EVI = ESI + 1 − ERI. Ecological resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics of a system state variable after a defined disturbance but is represented by indices including ecological elasticity limit and net primary production for terrestrial systems and water storage water transparency and chlorophyll a for aquatic systems. The paper states that it uses 11 indices selected from the twin perspectives of ecological sensitivity and ecological resilience and that all indices are normalized and assigned equal weight then summed to calculate ESI and ERI. Because resilience is derived from proxy indices rather than observed recovery trajectories the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The target variables used to quantify resilience are based on ecosystem composition and land cover classes in the ecological elasticity limit model with elasticities assigned to agricultural lands forests grasslands shrublands wetlands water bodies artificial surfaces and bare land. The analysis is conducted across Jiangsu province at a grid scale and uses remote sensing and statistical datasets which is field observation. The first author affiliation is in Nanjing China. The study area is Jiangsu province China with coordinates 30•45′N–35•20′N and 116•18′E–121•57′E and the provided location is the midpoint of that range. The framework explicitly separates aquatic and terrestrial indices and therefore involves multiple habitat types rather than a single dominant IUCN habitat type. No taxon is applicable because the resilience indicators are not organism specific and the level is landscape. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is inferred from indices rather than measured response to a disturbance.

The study states that a key feature of ecosystem response to disturbance is recovery time and that rapid recovery is associated with high resilience. It models postfire ecosystem recovery using satellite-derived observations of vegetation and uses a 10-y archive of biweekly MODIS NDVI imagery with millions of observations. Recovery time is defined as years for NDVI to return to prefire levels and is mapped and projected. The target variable used for recovery is NDVI which the paper links to biomass and describes as ecosystem-level above-ground biomass and fuel accumulation. The disturbance is fire and the analysis uses observed fire boundaries and burned area products to set postfire age with trajectories that reset after fire events indicating a pulse disturbance. The approach combines field observation via remote sensing and hierarchical Bayesian modeling with climate model projections. The study region is the Cape Floristic Region of South Africa and the maps show the region around 31 to 34 south and 18 to 24 east so the average coordinate is reported. The habitat is described as fynbos Mediterranean shrubland biome. The first author affiliation is University at Buffalo in the United States. The MODIS record is 2000 to 2010 which is 10 years for the observation window used for NDVI trajectories.

Framework: resilience = ability to absorb change without tipping
Justification: The study states it used a mathematical model of anoxic oxic regime shifts and simulated the ecosystem with ordinary differential equations. Resilience is defined in Box 1 as ability of the ecosystem to absorb a change in oxygen diffusivity without tipping to the alternative ecosystem state. The Methods describe that they ran simulations with a stepwise increase and a stepwise decrease of oxygen diffusivity and calculated tipping point positions. They quantified effect size as the difference in oxygen diffusivity between a tipping point without trait variation and with a given level of trait variation and note that a positive effect size indicates increased resilience. Because resilience is operationalised as the position of tipping points along the oxygen diffusivity driver this matches a threshold based quantification. The target variable used to quantify resilience is oxygen diffusivity at tipping points which is an abiotic parameter. The model ecosystem contains three functional groups of bacteria so the taxon is bacteria. The first author affiliation is University of Zurich in Switzerland. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is simulation based. The disturbance analysed is changing oxygen diffusivity which the text links to thermal stratification and anthropogenic increases of nutrients and temperature enhancing hypoxia so it is treated as a climatic driver in the model. The disturbance is applied as a stepwise gradient across 300 values rather than a discrete event so it is treated as press. The simulations ran 1000000 hours at each oxygen diffusivity level and there were 300 levels so the total simulated duration is 300000000 hours which is 41666667 days.

The study states it investigates predators mediating foundation species resistance to disturbance and recovery dynamics and it experimentally manipulated predator access using predator exclusion cages and unmanipulated controls. Resilience is quantified with direct field measurements of cordgrass border movement as area change per day reported as marsh area loss or gain in m2 per day and described as slower rates of border retreat and faster rates of lateral expansion. The framework is presented as resistance to disturbance and subsequent recovery in the introduction and the methods and discussion repeatedly treat resilience through resistance and recovery. Quantification is recovery rate because border movement is explicitly a rate in m2 per day during retreat and recovery phases. Target variables used to quantify resilience are cordgrass percent vegetation cover and cordgrass biomass and border movement of living vegetation. The focal organism is cordgrass Spartina alterniflora which is a plant and the measurements are at the population level as stem density biomass cover and border position are aggregated within plots. The disturbance includes drought described as drought induced die offs and the study period is described as a relatively dry period followed by a wetter period and the die offs formed during drought. The manipulated disturbance is biotic predator exclusion which changes predator access and thereby alters grazing pressure. The disturbance is treated as press because the study analyses periods of die off and regrowth across months with drought conditions spanning May 2016 to January 2017 and recovery February 2017 to August 2017 without a discrete event boundary. Observation duration spans from the first border delineation in May 2016 to the end of the recovery experiment in August 2017. The field site is given as Guana Tolomato Matanzas National Estuarine Research Reserve in Ponte Vedra Beach Florida USA with coordinates 30.02 N and minus 81.33 W. The first author affiliation is University of Florida Gainesville Florida USA.

The study states that ecosystem stability was assessed in terms of resistance and resilience and defines resilience as the ability to recover from disturbances and return to its original condition. It quantifies sensitivity and resilience of vegetation to short-term climate anomalies from 2000 to 2020 using an ARx model with standardized anomalies of NDVI temperature and precipitation. It states that the coefficient delta indicates how long it takes for the ecosystem to return to normal after being disrupted and that 1 minus delta was used to denote ecosystem resilience. Because resilience is derived from the NDVI time series dynamics and specifically from an autocorrelation type coefficient it is an observed measurement and matches recovery rate per the instructions that autocorrelation based measures fall under recovery rate. The target variable used in the resilience quantification is NDVI anomalies and NDVI is a vegetation index used as a proxy of productivity so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The analysis is based on satellite remote sensing and meteorological observations rather than imposed experimental disturbance so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation is Beijing Normal University in Beijing China so the institution country is CN. The study area is Duolun County Inner Mongolia China with coordinates 41 degrees 46 minutes to 42 degrees 36 minutes N and 115 degrees 51 minutes to 116 degrees 54 minutes E so the field country is CN and the average coordinate is N42.1833 E116.4167 with a location range coded as 0 because the span is under two degrees in both latitude and longitude. The study area is a transition zone between sandy land grassland and forest and the land use types analyzed include cropland woodland and grassland with the region described as typical grassland vegetation and meadow grassland vegetation so the dominant habitat is coded as grassland. The resilience concerns vegetation greenness dynamics so the taxon is Plantae. The disturbances are treated as climate anomalies and climate change perturbations without manipulation so the disturbance type is unmanipulated climatic and it is treated across the full 2000 to 2020 period so the pattern is press. The observation window for NDVI temperature and precipitation is 2000 to 2020 which is 21 years and is reported as 7670 days.

The study states that resistance is capacity to withstand change and resilience is capacity for recovery of function. It also states that following Ingrisch and Bahn 2018 they quantified resistance and resilience as a comparison between control and drought treatments in their experiment. It defines drought resistance as the difference between control and drought treatments during the seasonal drought treatment period and drought resilience as the differences magnitude and duration between control and drought treatments when seasonal drought treatments were not imposed legacy effects. The experiment manipulated precipitation with shelters excluding 66% of ambient precipitation in cool season drought and warm season drought treatments. The response variables measured include soil moisture phenology greenness and above ground biomass of tagged individuals of Achnatherum hymenoides and Pleuraphis jamesii. Because resilience is quantified from observed legacy differences between drought and control treatments after the drought period it is observed rather than inferred. The resilience quantification corresponds to recovery degree because it is based on the magnitude of difference from the control baseline during legacy periods rather than an explicit recovery rate or return time calculation. The target variables used for resilience quantification are above ground biomass and greenness based phenological metrics derived from greenness. The level is individual because biomass and phenology metrics were calculated for tagged individual plants. The approach is field experiment because precipitation was experimentally manipulated in 36 field plots at a site in southeastern Utah USA. The first author affiliation is USDA ARS in Fort Collins Colorado USA. The site coordinates are given as 38.19′N 109.75′W which converts to N38.3167 W109.7500. The drought treatments were applied over a 3 year period and the results section specifies the observation window from 29 April 2015 to 27 April 2018 which is 1095 days. The habitat is described as a native grass shrubland community and the dominant plant is a shrub Ephedra viridis so shrubland is the best match. The disturbance is manipulated drought which is a climatic disturbance and it is treated as a sustained seasonal precipitation reduction during each 6 month treatment period so press.

The introduction defines resilience as the ability to maintain and or return to its original state after disturbances and states that resilience is supported by two distinct mechanisms resistance and recovery. The study objective is to assess recovery potential and it develops a multi factor reef recovery index using TOPSIS rather than measuring recovery dynamics through time. The methods state that eight variables were selected based on documented relevance for reef recovery and expert opinions and were used in a multi criteria decision matrix to compute a relative proximity index RI. The variables used in the RI include coral species richness total and juvenile coral density hard coral cover proportion of stress tolerant coral species cover algal cover herbivorous fish biomass and sea surface temperature anomalies and recruitment rates for two islands. The discussion states the assessment is not based on temporally replicated time series and refers to the index as characterizing potential to recover from disturbance. Data were collected at reef sites in the South Western Indian Ocean including Reunion Rodrigues Mayotte Glorieuses and Europa with coordinates given for each island. The first author affiliation includes Universite de la Reunion in France. The habitat is coral reefs on outer slopes at 10 to 12 m depth which are shallow nearshore marine environments. Because resilience is quantified as an index derived from multiple indicators rather than observed recovery after a defined disturbance the measurement type is inferred and disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

Framework: resilience = how much change the system can withstand before flipping into another regime
Justification: The study proposes RID as the probability that the recovery threshold T2 is unattainable and it calculates T2 from a phosphorus driven eutrophication model with alternative stable states. Resilience is described as the original definition of resilience as how much change the system can withstand before flipping into another regime and the study focuses on thresholds for regime shifts and recovery. RID is computed from Bayesian posterior parameter samples and thresholds and the paper also runs dynamic simulations with load reduction and white noise. The state variable in the model is water phosphorus concentration P and the control variable is external phosphorus load lP so the quantified resilience related quantity is a threshold in environmental pressure. The disturbance is implemented in simulations as long term lP reduction as press and random noise as pulses but the irreversible degradation definition is with respect to long term external drivers also known as press. The first author affiliation is Nanyang Technological University Singapore. Data are based on Lake Erhai in Southeast China. Lake Erhai is a freshwater lake so the habitat type is Wetland. No organism group is the target because the quantified variable is phosphorus concentration. The location is approximated from Lake Erhai in Yunnan China. The methods describe using water quality and lP data and long iterations for stable states so an exact observation window is not stated and a multi year monitoring context is implied so the duration is set to 3650 days.

The study quantified resilience using Landsat time series and LandTrendr by monitoring NDVI trajectories from 1984 to 2022 and extracting recovery time disturbance magnitude and recovery magnitude. It defined resilience as the ability of an ecosystem to recover to a predisturbance stable state after a disturbance. The vegetation resilience indicator was calculated from a recovery curve built with recovery time as the horizontal coordinate and disturbance magnitude as the vertical coordinate and the curve was integrated over recovery time 1 to 25 years. Recovery time was defined as the time required for the open pit mine to recover to the state before the disturbance and was directly used in the resilience calculation. NDVI was the observed variable used to infer disturbance and recovery and NDVI is a vegetation index used as a proxy of productivity so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The study area was around Dongting Lake in the Yangtze River Economic Belt in China and the mines were around Dongting Lake. The first author affiliation was China University of Geosciences Beijing in China. The disturbance was open pit mining and restoration activities described as drivers of vegetation change and represented as event changes with phases and years of events in the NDVI trajectory which matches a pulse framing. The observation window used all available Landsat images for June to September 1984 to 2022 which spans 38 years. The habitat context emphasized Dongting Lake wetland ecosystems and the study area is around Dongting Lake. The resilience quantified was vegetation resilience so the taxon is plants.

The paper states that resilience indicators indirectly probe resilience by measuring relative change in recovery back to equilibrium after a disturbance and it links this to critical slowing down and slow recovery. It quantifies indicators from simulated time series using variance and autocorrelation at lag 1 and it also discusses recovery rate as exponential convergence governed by the dominant eigenvalue. The Methods simulate a stochastic Lotka Volterra competition model and compute standard deviation and autocorrelation at lag 1 from species biomasses and from total community biomass. The study also computes a community multivariate variability index from the variance covariance matrix of species biomasses. The disturbance is imposed in the model by gradually changing environmental conditions through parameter M described as a stressor integrating changes in climate harvest rates nutrient levels or habitat quality and it is ramped up in steps. This is a press disturbance because M is gradually increased and indicators are estimated for multiple levels prior to the transition without a defined recovery interval. The target variable used for indicator estimation is species biomass and total community biomass. The level is coded as community because the main comparison emphasizes community level indicators derived from total species biomass and a community multivariate index. The approach is modeling and simulation because all analyses are based on simulated time series from the stochastic model.
